title: Check if string is palindrome
description: Check whether a string is palindrome using recursion

examples.explanation:
1. Call is_palindrome("racecar") → length=7 > 1, first='r', last='r' match
2. Recursive call is_palindrome("aceca") → length=5 > 1, first='a', last='a' match
3. Recursive call is_palindrome("cec") → length=3 > 1, first='c', last='c' match
4. Recursive call is_palindrome("e") → length=1 ≤ 1, return true
5. Return true up the chain → final result: true

1. Call is_palindrome("hello") → length=5 > 1, first='h', last='o' don't match
2. Return false immediately → final result: false

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursive O(n²)
approaches.summary: Recursively check if the string is a palindrome by comparing the first and last characters, then recursively calling on the substring without them. This approach is straightforward but inefficient due to substring creation.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive Palindrome Check with Substring Creation
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution that checks palindrome by comparing first/last characters and recursing on substring.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the palindrome checking problem using pure recursion with substring creation. The intuition is that a string is a palindrome if its first and last characters are equal AND the substring between them is also a palindrome. This creates a recursive definition that naturally decomposes the problem. The algorithm works by: 1) Handling base cases (empty string or single character are always palindromes), 2) Comparing the first and last characters, 3) If they match, recursively checking the substring excluding these two characters, 4) Returning false immediately if any comparison fails. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. If length ≤ 1: return true (base case) 2. If first_char != last_char: return false 3. Return recursive_call(substring[1:-1]). Time complexity is O(n²) because each recursive call creates a new substring of O(n) length, and there are O(n) recursive calls. Space complexity is O(n²) due to storing all these substrings on the call stack. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive logic that uses substring extraction as a key operation. Common mistakes include: forgetting base cases, incorrect substring indices (off-by-one errors), and not handling empty strings properly. Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(s): if len(s) <= 1: return True; if s[0] != s[-1]: return False; return is_palindrome(s[1:-1]).

examples.trace:
1. Call is_palindrome("racecar") → length=7 > 1, first='r', last='r' match
2. Recursive call is_palindrome("aceca") → length=5 > 1, first='a', last='a' match
3. Recursive call is_palindrome("cec") → length=3 > 1, first='c', last='c' match
4. Recursive call is_palindrome("e") → length=1 ≤ 1, return true
5. Return true up the chain → final result: true

1. Call is_palindrome("hello") → length=5 > 1, first='h', last='o' don't match
2. Return false immediately → final result: false

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Palindrome Check with Index Parameters
ladders.desc: Recursive function that checks palindrome using start/end indices instead of substring creation.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive logic without the inefficiency of substring creation. The intuition is that we can check palindrome status by comparing characters at moving indices without creating new strings. This is more efficient than the full problem's approach. The algorithm: 1. Base case: if start ≥ end, return true (empty or single character), 2. If s[start] != s[end], return false, 3. Recursively call with start+1 and end-1. Time complexity is O(n) for n/2 recursive calls, each with O(1) work. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This connects to L0 by providing the recursive logic that L0 would use if it passed indices instead of substrings. Common mistakes: incorrect base case condition (should be ≥ not >), forgetting to increment/decrement indices. Real code pattern: def is_palindrome_helper(s, start, end): if start >= end: return True; if s[start] != s[end]: return False; return is_palindrome_helper(s, start+1, end-1).

examples.trace:
1. Call helper("radar", 0, 4) → s[0]='r', s[4]='r' match
2. Recursive call helper("radar", 1, 3) → s[1]='a', s[3]='a' match
3. Recursive call helper("radar", 2, 2) → start=2, end=2, start ≥ end, return true
4. Return true up the chain → final result: true

1. Call helper("test", 0, 3) → s[0]='t', s[3]='t' match
2. Recursive call helper("test", 1, 2) → s[1]='e', s[2]='s' don't match
3. Return false immediately → final result: false

ladders.title: Core Logic — Character Comparison with Moving Indices
ladders.desc: The technique of comparing characters from both ends while moving indices toward the center.
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the fundamental palindrome checking technique without recursion. The intuition is that a palindrome reads the same forward and backward, so we can verify this by comparing pairs of characters from the ends moving inward. The algorithm: 1. Initialize left=0, right=len(s)-1, 2. While left < right: compare s[left] and s[right], if not equal return false, increment left, decrement right, 3. If loop completes, return true. Time complexity is O(n) with n/2 comparisons. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 by providing the iterative version of the same logic that L1 implements recursively. Common mistakes: using ≤ instead of < in loop condition (would overcompare), not handling empty string properly. Real code pattern: left, right = 0, len(s)-1; while left < right: if s[left] != s[right]: return False; left += 1; right -= 1; return True.

examples.trace:
1. left=0, right=4 → compare 'l' and 'l' match
2. left=1, right=3 → compare 'e' and 'e' match
3. left=2, right=2 → loop condition left < right is false, exit
4. Return true

1. left=0, right=5 → compare 'p' and 'n' don't match
2. Return false immediately

ladders.title: Building Block — String Indexing and Slicing
ladders.desc: Accessing characters by index and extracting substrings in Python-like syntax.
ladders.explanation: This building block covers how to access individual characters and create substrings, which is essential for the recursive approach. The intuition is that strings are sequences where we can access elements by position (indexing) and extract contiguous parts (slicing). Key operations: 1. Indexing: s[i] gets character at position i (0-based, negative indices count from end), 2. Slicing: s[start:end] gets substring from start to end-1, s[start:] from start to end, s[:end] from beginning to end-1, s[start:end:step] with step. Time complexity: indexing is O(1), slicing is O(k) where k is length of slice. Space complexity: slicing creates new string of O(k) space. This connects to L2 by providing the operations needed to implement character comparison and substring extraction. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in slicing, confusing negative indices. Real code pattern: first_char = s[0]; last_char = s[-1]; middle = s[1:-1].

examples.input: s="algorithm", get first, last, and substring without them
examples.trace:
1. s[0] = 'a'
2. s[-1] = 'm'
3. s[1:-1] = characters from index 1 to second-to-last = 'lgorith'

1. s[1:3] = indices 1,2 = 'es'
2. s[:2] = indices 0,1 = 'te'
3. s[2:] = indices 2 to end = 'st'

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Equality Comparison
ladders.desc: Comparing if two characters are equal using == operator.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is the fundamental comparison needed for palindrome checking. The intuition is simple: two characters are equal if they represent the same letter/symbol. In programming, characters are compared using the equality operator (==), which returns true if they match exactly (case-sensitive). The algorithm: given two characters a and b, return a == b. Time and space complexity are both O(1). This connects to L3 by being the comparison operation used when accessing characters via indexing. Common mistakes: using = (assignment) instead of == (comparison), not considering case sensitivity. Real code pattern: if char1 == char2: return True else: return False.

examples.trace: 1. 'a' == 'a' → true
1. 'A' == 'a' → false (different cases)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understanding the definition of a palindrome string.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept defines what a palindrome is, which is prerequisite for solving the problem. A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards. The intuition comes from symmetry: if you reverse the string, you get the same string. Examples: 'racecar' reversed is 'racecar', 'hello' reversed is 'olleh' (not same). Formal definition: For string s of length n, s is palindrome if s[i] = s[n-1-i] for all i from 0 to n-1. Special cases: empty string and single character strings are trivially palindromes. This connects to L4 by establishing what we're trying to verify with character comparisons. Common mistakes: thinking palindromes must have odd length (false: 'noon' has even length), confusing with other concepts like anagrams. Real code pattern: Understanding only, no code needed.

examples.trace:
1. Forward: m-a-d-a-m
2. Backward: m-a-d-a-m
3. Same → yes

1. Forward: w-o-r-l-d
2. Backward: d-l-r-o-w
3. Different → no

approaches.name: Two Pointer Recursive O(n)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with two pointers (indices) to compare characters from the start and end of the string, moving inward without modifying the string. This avoids substring overhead and is more efficient.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive Two-Pointer Palindrome Check
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution comparing characters from both ends without string modification
ladders.explanation: This solution implements a recursive palindrome check using two pointers that start at the beginning and end of the string, moving inward with each recursive call. The intuition is that a string is a palindrome if its first and last characters match AND the substring between them is also a palindrome. By using indices instead of creating substrings, we avoid O(n²) time complexity from substring operations. The algorithm begins with pointers at indices 0 and len(s)-1. At each step, we check if left >= right (base case - empty or single character substring is always palindrome), then check if characters at these indices match. If they don't match, return False. If they match, recursively call with left+1 and right-1. Time complexity is O(n) where n is string length, as each character is visited once. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive function that calls the helper. Common mistakes include forgetting base cases, using substring operations instead of indices, and not handling empty strings properly. Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(s, left=0, right=None): if right is None: right = len(s)-1; if left >= right: return True; if s[left] != s[right]: return False; return is_palindrome(s, left+1, right-1)

examples.trace:
Initial call: is_palindrome("racecar", 0, 6)
Step 1: left=0, right=6, s[0]='r', s[6]='r' match → recursive call with left=1, right=5
Step 2: left=1, right=5, s[1]='a', s[5]='a' match → recursive call with left=2, right=4
Step 3: left=2, right=4, s[2]='c', s[4]='c' match → recursive call with left=3, right=3
Step 4: left=3, right=3, left >= right → return True
Final: True

Initial call: is_palindrome("hello", 0, 4)
Step 1: left=0, right=4, s[0]='h', s[4]='o' don't match → return False
Final: False

testCases.input: "A man a plan a canal Panama"

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Character Comparison Function
ladders.desc: Recursive function that compares characters at given indices and recurses inward
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that takes a string and two indices, comparing characters and making recursive calls. The intuition is that palindrome checking can be broken down into comparing outermost characters and checking the inner substring recursively. The algorithm: function compare(s, left, right): if left >= right return True (base case); if s[left] != s[right] return False; return compare(s, left+1, right-1). Time complexity is O(n) where n is the distance between pointers. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. This connects to L0 as the main recursive engine - L0 adds the wrapper that sets initial indices. Common mistakes include incorrect base case conditions (should be left >= right, not left == right), off-by-one errors with indices, and not returning the recursive result. Real code pattern: def compare_chars(s, left, right): if left >= right: return True; if s[left] != s[right]: return False; return compare_chars(s, left+1, right-1)

examples.trace:
Call: compare_chars("racecar", 0, 6)
Step 1: left=0, right=6, s[0]='r', s[6]='r' match → recursive call with left=1, right=5
Step 2: left=1, right=5, s[1]='a', s[5]='a' match → recursive call with left=2, right=4
Step 3: left=2, right=4, s[2]='c', s[4]='c' match → recursive call with left=3, right=3
Step 4: left=3, right=3, left >= right → return True
Final: True

Call: compare_chars("hello", 1, 3)
Step 1: left=1, right=3, s[1]='e', s[3]='l' don't match → return False
Final: False

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Symmetry Checking
ladders.desc: The insight that palindrome checking reduces to checking symmetry recursively
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core recursive insight: a string is a palindrome if its first and last characters are equal AND the substring between them is also a palindrome. The intuition is recursive decomposition - breaking a problem into smaller identical subproblems. For palindrome "racecar", we check: 'r' == 'r' AND "aceca" is palindrome. Then "aceca" checks: 'a' == 'a' AND "cec" is palindrome. This continues until we reach base cases. The algorithm mentally: is_palindrome(s) = (s[0] == s[-1]) AND is_palindrome(s[1:-1]). Time complexity analysis shows each recursive call reduces problem size by 2, leading to O(n) calls. Space complexity is O(n) for call stack. This connects to L1 as the conceptual foundation for the recursive function. Common mistakes include not understanding the recursive decomposition, trying to check entire string at once instead of recursively, and confusion about base cases. Real code pattern represents the mental model: def is_palindrome(s): if len(s) <= 1: return True; return s[0] == s[-1] and is_palindrome(s[1:-1])

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check "abba" → 'a' == 'a'? Yes AND is_palindrome("bb")
Step 2: Check "bb" → 'b' == 'b'? Yes AND is_palindrome("")
Step 3: Check "" → len <= 1 → return True
Step 4: Backtrack: "bb" returns True, "abba" returns True
Final: True

Step 1: Check "abc" → 'a' == 'c'? No → return False
Final: False

ladders.title: Building Block — String Index Manipulation with Recursion
ladders.desc: Using indices to traverse string recursively without creating substrings
ladders.explanation: This level teaches how to use indices with recursion to avoid expensive substring operations. The intuition is that instead of creating new strings (which takes O(n) time and space each time), we can pass indices that point to positions in the original string. This is crucial for efficiency. The algorithm: function traverse(s, start, end): if start >= end return; else process s[start] and s[end]; traverse(s, start+1, end-1). Time complexity for traversal is O(n) where n is distance between indices. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. This connects to L2 by providing the implementation technique for the recursive symmetry check. Common mistakes include confusing string slicing with index manipulation, off-by-one errors with index arithmetic, and not understanding that indices represent a window into the original string. Real code pattern: def traverse_indices(s, left, right): if left >= right: return; print(s[left], s[right]); traverse_indices(s, left+1, right-1)

examples.trace:
Call: traverse_indices("hello", 0, 4)
Step 1: left=0, right=4 → print s[0]='h', s[4]='o'
Step 2: recursive call with left=1, right=3
Step 3: left=1, right=3 → print s[1]='e', s[3]='l'
Step 4: recursive call with left=2, right=2
Step 5: left=2, right=2 → left >= right → return
Final output shows paired characters

Call: traverse_indices("abc", 0, 2)
Step 1: left=0, right=2 → print s[0]='a', s[2]='c'
Step 2: recursive call with left=1, right=1
Step 3: left=1, right=1 → left >= right → return
Final output shows paired characters

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison and Index Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Comparing characters at specific indices and calculating next indices
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operations needed: accessing string characters by index, comparing two characters, and performing index arithmetic. The intuition is that strings are indexed sequences where s[i] gives the character at position i. Comparing s[left] and s[right] checks if characters match. Index arithmetic (left+1, right-1) moves pointers inward. The algorithm: access s[left] and s[right]; compare them using ==; if equal, prepare next indices: new_left = left+1, new_right = right-1. Time complexity is O(1) per operation. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as the atomic operations used in recursive traversal. Common mistakes include confusing 0-based indexing (first character at index 0), forgetting that string indices must be within bounds, and incorrect arithmetic when indices cross. Real code pattern: char1 = s[left]; char2 = s[right]; if char1 == char2: next_left = left+1; next_right = right-1

examples.output: chars: 'h' and 'o', equal: False, next_left=1, next_right=3
examples.trace:
Step 1: Access s[0] = 'h'
Step 2: Access s[4] = 'o'
Step 3: Compare 'h' == 'o' → False
Step 4: Calculate next_left = 0+1 = 1
Step 5: Calculate next_right = 4-1 = 3
Final: Characters don't match, next indices would be 1 and 3

examples.output: chars: 'r' and 'r', equal: True, next_left=1, next_right=5
examples.trace:
Step 1: Access s[0] = 'r'
Step 2: Access s[6] = 'r'
Step 3: Compare 'r' == 'r' → True
Step 4: Calculate next_left = 0+1 = 1
Step 5: Calculate next_right = 6-1 = 5
Final: Characters match, next indices would be 1 and 5

testCases.expected: chars: 'h' and 'o', equal: False, next_left=1, next_right=3
chars: 'r' and 'r', equal: True, next_left=1, next_right=5
chars: 'a' and 'b', equal: False, next_left=1, next_right=0
chars: 'a' and 'a', equal: True, next_left=1, next_right=0
chars: 'b' and 'b', equal: True, next_left=2, next_right=0
chars: 'e' and 's', equal: False, next_left=2, next_right=1
chars: 'd' and 'd', equal: True, next_left=3, next_right=1
chars: 'e' and 'e', equal: True, next_left=2, next_right=2
chars: 't' and 'h', equal: False, next_left=3, next_right=2
chars: 'a' and 'a', equal: True, next_left=1, next_right=-1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Indexing and Basic Recursion
ladders.desc: Understanding string as indexable sequence and simple recursive function calls
ladders.explanation: This foundational level covers two prerequisites: 1) Strings as indexable sequences where each character has a position starting from 0, and 2) Basic recursion where a function calls itself with modified parameters. The intuition for string indexing: "hello" has indices 0:'h', 1:'e', 2:'l', 3:'l', 4:'o'. We can access s[0] to get 'h'. For recursion: a recursive function has a base case (stopping condition) and recursive case (calls itself with simpler input). Example: def countdown(n): if n <= 0: return; else: print(n); countdown(n-1). Time complexity depends on recursion depth. Space complexity is O(n) for call stack. This connects to L4 as the prerequisite knowledge for character access and recursive structure. Common mistakes include 1-based indexing confusion, not understanding that strings are immutable, recursion without base case (infinite recursion), and not modifying parameters in recursive call. Real code pattern for string access: s = "hello"; first_char = s[0]; last_char = s[len(s)-1]. For recursion: def recursive_func(param): if base_case: return result; else: return recursive_func(modified_param)

examples.trace:
String "hello" has 5 characters with indices 0-4:
Index 0 → character 'h'
Index 2 → character 'l'
Index 4 → character 'o'
All accesses are valid within range 0 to len(s)-1

Initial call: countdown(3)
Step 1: n=3, not <=0 → print 3, call countdown(2)
Step 2: n=2, not <=0 → print 2, call countdown(1)
Step 3: n=1, not <=0 → print 1, call countdown(0)
Step 4: n=0, <=0 → return
Execution completes with printed values 3,2,1

testCases.expected: IndexError: string index out of range
IndexError: string index out of range

approaches.name: Tail Recursion Optimized O(n)
approaches.summary: Implement recursion as tail-recursive by passing the string and updated pointers, allowing some compilers to optimize it to iterative space usage. This is similar to two-pointer but designed for potential space optimization.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Tail Recursive Palindrome Check
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of palindrome checking using tail recursion with pointer optimization
ladders.explanation: This solution implements a palindrome check using tail recursion, where the recursive call is the last operation in the function. The key insight is that by passing the string along with updated start and end pointers as parameters, we can structure the recursion so that it doesn't need to maintain a call stack for returning results. This allows compilers that support tail call optimization (TCO) to transform the recursion into an iterative loop, achieving O(1) space complexity instead of O(n). The algorithm works by comparing characters at the current start and end positions. If they match, it makes a recursive call with incremented start and decremented end pointers. The base cases are: when start >= end (empty string or single character), return true; when characters don't match, return false immediately. The intuition is that we're essentially performing a two-pointer comparison but using recursion as the control flow mechanism. This approach is particularly elegant because it maintains the recursive thinking pattern while potentially achieving iterative space efficiency through compiler optimization. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle empty strings, not properly updating pointers, or structuring the recursion in a non-tail position. The connection to L1 is that this full solution uses the tail recursive helper function as its core component.

examples.trace:
1. Initial call: is_palindrome_tail("racecar", 0, 6)
2. Compare s[0]='r' and s[6]='r' → match
3. Recursive call: is_palindrome_tail("racecar", 1, 5)
4. Compare s[1]='a' and s[5]='a' → match
5. Recursive call: is_palindrome_tail("racecar", 2, 4)
6. Compare s[2]='c' and s[4]='c' → match
7. Recursive call: is_palindrome_tail("racecar", 3, 3)
8. Base case: start=3, end=3 → start >= end → return True
9. Unwind: True propagates back through all calls
Final result: True

1. Initial call: is_palindrome_tail("hello", 0, 4)
2. Compare s[0]='h' and s[4]='o' → mismatch
3. Immediate return: False
Final result: False

testCases.input: "A man a plan a canal Panama"

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Tail Recursive Helper Function
ladders.desc: The tail recursive function that compares characters and recurses with updated pointers
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive function that implements the palindrome checking logic. It takes three parameters: the string s, and integer pointers start and end representing the current positions to compare. The function is designed to be tail recursive, meaning the recursive call is the last operation executed before returning. This is achieved by having the return statement be simply the recursive call result (when characters match) or an immediate false (when they don't). The base case occurs when start >= end, which means we've compared all necessary character pairs (including the case of empty string or single character). When characters at start and end match, we make a recursive call with start+1 and end-1. The intuition is that we're progressively moving toward the center of the string, comparing symmetric positions. This function is tail recursive because no computation happens after the recursive call returns - the result is simply passed back up the call chain. Time complexity is O(n) as we make at most n/2 recursive calls. Space complexity is O(n) without optimization, but O(1) with tail call optimization. Common mistakes include incorrect base case conditions, off-by-one errors in pointer updates, or not handling the empty string case properly. The connection to L0 is that this function is the complete implementation once wrapped with an initial call.

examples.trace:
1. Call: helper("noon", 0, 3)
2. Compare s[0]='n' and s[3]='n' → match
3. Recursive call: helper("noon", 1, 2)
4. Compare s[1]='o' and s[2]='o' → match
5. Recursive call: helper("noon", 2, 1)
6. Base case: start=2, end=1 → start >= end → return True
7. Unwind: True propagates back
Final result: True

1. Call: helper("world", 0, 4)
2. Compare s[0]='w' and s[4]='d' → mismatch
3. Immediate return: False
Final result: False

ladders.title: Core Logic — Character Comparison with Pointer Movement
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of comparing symmetric characters and moving toward the center
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core algorithmic pattern: compare characters at symmetric positions, then move inward. Given a string s and indices i and j, we compare s[i] and s[j]. If they're equal, we continue checking with i+1 and j-1. If they're different, we immediately know the string isn't a palindrome. The intuition is that a palindrome reads the same forward and backward, so characters at symmetric positions must be identical. We start with i=0 and j=len(s)-1, comparing the outermost characters, then progressively move toward the center. The process stops when i >= j, meaning we've checked all necessary pairs (when i and j cross or meet). This is essentially a two-pointer approach, but implemented recursively. The key insight is that we don't need to compare every character to every other character - we only need to compare each character to its symmetric counterpart. This reduces the number of comparisons from O(n²) to O(n). Time complexity is O(n) for n/2 comparisons. Space complexity depends on implementation but is O(1) for the comparison logic itself. Common mistakes include comparing characters case-sensitively when not intended, or not handling special characters properly. The connection to L1 is that this comparison and pointer update logic forms the body of the recursive function.

examples.trace:
1. Compare s[0]='r' and s[4]='r' → equal
2. Update: i becomes 1, j becomes 3
3. Next comparison will be s[1]='a' and s[3]='a'

1. Compare s[0]='h' and s[4]='o' → not equal
2. Immediate conclusion: not a palindrome
3. No further comparisons needed

ladders.title: Building Block — String Indexing and Length Calculation
ladders.desc: Accessing characters by position and determining string boundaries
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operations needed to work with strings in the palindrome algorithm: accessing characters by index and determining the valid index range. String indexing allows us to access individual characters using their position (0-based index). For a string of length n, valid indices are 0 through n-1. The length operation gives us n, which helps determine the initial end pointer as n-1. The intuition is that we need to be able to reference specific positions in the string to compare them. In Python-style syntax, s[i] gives the character at position i, and len(s) gives the string length. Understanding zero-based indexing is crucial: the first character is at index 0, the last at index len(s)-1. When implementing the palindrome check, we start by comparing s[0] and s[len(s)-1], then s[1] and s[len(s)-2], and so on. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing, accessing indices outside the valid range, or misunderstanding how len() works with empty strings (len("") = 0). Time complexity for indexing is O(1) in most languages. Space complexity is O(1) for the operations themselves. The connection to L2 is that these operations enable the character comparisons at symmetric positions.

examples.trace:
1. String "hello" has indices: 0='h', 1='e', 2='l', 3='l', 4='o'
2. s[1] accesses index 1
3. Character at index 1 is 'e'
Result: 'e'

1. String "world" has 5 characters
2. len(s) = 5
3. Last index = len(s) - 1 = 5 - 1 = 4
4. Valid indices: 0,1,2,3,4
Result: end index = 4

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Equality Comparison
ladders.desc: Comparing two characters for exact equality
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental operation in palindrome checking: determining if two characters are identical. Character comparison is typically done using the equality operator (== in most languages). Characters are compared based on their Unicode code points, so 'A' and 'a' are different unless case normalization is applied. The intuition is simple: for a string to be a palindrome, characters at symmetric positions must be exactly the same. This operation returns True if the characters are identical, False otherwise. In the context of palindrome checking, we compare s[start] and s[end] at each step. It's important to understand that character comparison is different from string comparison - we're comparing individual characters, not substrings. Time complexity is O(1) as it's a simple comparison operation. Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes include confusing assignment (=) with comparison (==), or not considering case sensitivity when it matters. Some implementations might need to handle spaces or punctuation by skipping them, but at this basic level, we're just comparing the characters as given. The connection to L3 is that once we can access characters by index, we need to compare them to determine if they match.

examples.trace:
1. Take first character: 'a'
2. Take second character: 'a'
3. Compare using == operator
4. 'a' == 'a' evaluates to True
Result: True

1. Take first character: 'b'
2. Take second character: 'c'
3. Compare using == operator
4. 'b' == 'c' evaluates to False
Result: False

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understanding the definition and basic examples of palindromes
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a sequence of characters that reads the same forward and backward, ignoring spaces, punctuation, and capitalization in some definitions, but for our basic definition, we consider exact character matching. The word comes from Greek 'palin' (again) and 'dromos' (way, direction). The intuition is symmetry: if you reverse the sequence, you get exactly the same sequence. Simple examples include single characters (like 'a'), empty strings (technically palindromes by definition), 'racecar', 'madam', 'noon', and 'abba'. Non-examples include 'hello', 'world', 'abc'. Understanding palindromes is fundamental because it establishes what property we're trying to verify. In computer science terms, a string s is a palindrome if s == s[::-1] (where [::-1] means reversed). However, we want to check this more efficiently without creating a reversed copy. The key insight for algorithms is that we only need to compare the first half of the string to the second half in reverse order. Common misconceptions include thinking palindromes must be words (they can be any sequence, like '12321'), or forgetting that empty strings and single characters are palindromes by definition. This foundation connects to L4 because once we understand what a palindrome is, we know we need to compare characters for equality at symmetric positions.

examples.trace:
1. Read forward: r-a-c-e-c-a-r
2. Read backward: r-a-c-e-c-a-r
3. Both readings are identical
4. Therefore, "racecar" is a palindrome

1. Read forward: h-e-l-l-o
2. Read backward: o-l-l-e-h
3. Forward reading ≠ backward reading
4. Therefore, "hello" is not a palindrome
